  • Hide All Code
  • Download Rmd

Exploring SOEP Data

Exploring SOEP data

Model

\[ log(income_{i,t+5})=\beta_0+\beta_1coresidence_it+\beta_2X_{it}+\epsilon_{it} \]

\[\beta_1\] is likely biased, because parental co-residence during college years is not random

Variables of interest

In this Notebook, I explore different aspects of the SOEP household survey data set from DIW. My aim is to find longitudinal information on the following five variables:

  1. Age

  2. Income

  3. Study grant received, specifically BAföG (Bundesausbildungsförderungsgesetz)

  4. Parental co-residence

  5. Student enrollment

  6. Family income

  7. Level/duration of education

Let’s start by loading the harmonized data sets:

1: Age

3: Study grant received

4. Parental co-residence

I should look into the jump before 2010. Is that because many older people were added then? Or perhaps because of some kind of harmonization issue? Consider household roster measure if this one is not reliable.

5. Student enrolment

Check whether this downward trend is because of changes to the underlying survey data.

6. Parental income

7. Number of years in education

Combining variables

Tests:

  1. Check missing values for all variables

  2. Construct a cohort that I am interested in (pre-BAföG reform and post-BAföG reform)

  3. Association: Check the correlation between parental co-residence and later earnings)

  4. Check instrument strength (relation between reform and parental coresidence)

[1] FALSE
[1] FALSE

Missing values

High missing values for the grant are to be expected, because the grant question is only asked to people who are students. High missings for age and student in final years are strange.

syear missing_income missing_coresidence missing_grant missing_student missing_age missing_income_parent missing_educ

Construct cohort

25th BAföG Amendment (25. BAföGÄndG) — Key Features

Most changes took effect on 1 August 2016 for the winter semester 2016/17 (the main BAföG period in Germany).

Descriptive statistics of these students.

I’m only getting around 200 students who were students for the first time in a given academic year. I wonder if I need more for (a) the analysis and also (b) if the student variable is biased downwards.

syear n income coresidence grant student age

Graphical visualization of the missings:

You can use BAföG receipt as a consistency check.

If BAföG recipients >> students classified
→ your student definition is too strict.

[1] 421

Association: Check the correlation between parental co-residence and later earnings)

Now combining later income and parental co-residence to calculate the correlation:

Simple DiD approach:

  • Outcome variable: parental co-residence

  • Treatment group: students with high parental income

  • Control group: students with low parental income

  • Time window: 2014, 2018

[1] -0.1266649


===============================================
                        Dependent variable:    
                    ---------------------------
                                 y             
-----------------------------------------------
treated                                        
                                               
                                               
post                          -0.005           
                              (0.019)          
                                               
treated:post                                   
                                               
                                               
Constant                     0.668***          
                              (0.014)          
                                               
-----------------------------------------------
Observations                   2,412           
R2                            0.00002          
Adjusted R2                   -0.0004          
Residual Std. Error      0.472 (df = 2410)     
F Statistic            0.058 (df = 1; 2410)    
===============================================
Note:               *p<0.1; **p<0.05; ***p<0.01

Approach with all young people (and not just students)

Naive model

\[ log(income_i)=\beta_0+\beta_1coresidence_i+\beta_2X_i+\epsilon_i \]

where:

  • \(log(income_i)\) is the income of person \(i\) when they are 30 years old;
  • \(coresidence_i\) is the number of years that person \(i\) lived independently from 18 to 25;
  • \(X_i\) are control variables;
  • \(\epsilon_i\) is the error term.

Stylized facts

Fraction of young people living with their parents

Income distribution at 30 years

Differences in parental income

Naive estimation

For the naive estimation, we construct the following variables:

  • Outcome variable: log wages at age 30

  • Treatment variable: parental co-residence, number of years a person lived with their parents between 18 and 30 (not including 30)

  • Covariates:

    • Parental income: average log wages of parents when the person was between 18 and 30 years old

    • Years of schooling: number of years of schooling, measured at 30 years old

Error in model.frame.default(formula = income ~ coresidence, data = df_naive,  : 
  invalid type (list) for variable 'income'

Standard estimation Mincer earnings equation

Necessary variables

  • Wage (hourly, monthly, or annual)

  • Years of schooling

  • Potential experience

Data set

Removing missing values

Summary statistics

Estimation of the basic Mincer equation
